EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes region on the morning of January 29 and departed into Canada. In the wake of this low, an arctic cold front moved through the lower Great Lakes region early on January 29, allowing for arctic high pressure to build over the midwest, setting up an extremely cold pattern for January 29 - 31. The blistering cold that entered the region resulted in temperatures on January 30 and 31 being some of the coldest recorded in five years. A strong pressure gradient set up over the region between the departing low pressure system and the incoming surface high resulting in winds to 30 - 35 mph across the region. These strong winds along with the extreme cold allowed for wind chills to drop below zero degrees by midday on January 29 and not return above zero until late on January 31. The coldest temperatures during this event were on the morning of January 30 with several locations approaching or exceeding record low temperatures in the -5F to -11F range. During these coldest temperatures, the wind chill reached -25F for several hours across the entire area with several locations reaching wind chill values of -35F to -40F for a time. Temperatures on January 30 stayed near or below zero for much of the day and dropped back below zero on the morning of January 31. Record low temperatures were set again on January 31, but the winds had subsided to 15 to 20 mph across the area, allowing for wind chills in the -15F to -20F range. Temperatures reached the upper single digits during the day on January 31 and winds subsided to light and variable, ending the extreme cold threat. Four deaths were reported in Ohio as a result of the extreme cold. All area schools closed on both January 30 and 31 due to the cold conditions.

All area schools were closed on January 30 and 31 due to the extreme cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A panhandle hook low pressure system moved northeast into the Great Lakes region on December 31, deepening as it moved north of Lake Erie on the evening of the 31st. This low extended a cold front across northeast Ohio, early on the 1st. Strong winds along and behind this front downed trees and power lines across the area, prompting widespread power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An automated weather station at Akron-Canton Regional Airport measured a wind gust of 53 mph. Another automated weather station at Akron Fulton Airport measured a wind gust of 48 mph. Several trees were reported downed in the northern part of the county, specifically, in Northfield and Twinsburg. Tens of thousands were without power into the morning of the 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked through the central Great Lakes bringing widespread rain to the area along with temperatures in the mid 40s.  This warm up was preceded by a significant snow event on the 19th and 20th which produced widespread snow amounts from 6 to 16 inches. Temperatures then dropped below freezing reaching as low as 10 degrees below zero on the 21st. This cold snap resulted in ice growth on area rivers. By the 23rd the ground had frozen and the snowpack remained dense. The rain on the 23rd into the 24th was between 0.5 and 1.5 inches, heaviest in the areas of highest snowpack. The majority of the snowpack melted, contributing 0.5-0.75 inches of runoff.  Ten river forecast points exceeded flood stage, with the Scioto River at La Rue reaching moderate flood stage.  Several roads flooded across the region, though mainly in typical low water crossing. At least two ice jams developed that resulted in flooding in evacuations; one at the Vermilion River in Vermilion, the other on the Chagrin River in Eastlake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Eagle Creek in Findlay reached a stage of 10.9 feet midday on the 24th.  Flooding was reported but impacts were dampened due to the fact that the main stem Blanchard River was not in flood.  Flood waters inundated portions of South Blanchard Street, Brookside Drive, Fremont and Graceland Streets. The intersections of Beechmont Drive with Fairmont and Rosemont Drives also flooded.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: River ice estimated between 6 and 8 inches thick developed along the Vermilion River during a cold snap around the 21st. Rain and snowmelt on the 23rd caused the river ice to break up and jam. The jam was reported by local authorities to the NWS around 1025 PM EST. By 1144 PM evacuations were underway with swift water rescue teams. In all 11 people were rescued including children. There were at least 40 homes impacted by the ice and flood waters which measured 2 to 5 feet deep.  Reports from a resident that there were no flood waters at 10 pm, 2 feet of water starting encroaching on homes by 1030 pm, and by 11 pm had doubled to 4 feet deep.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of rain, snowmelt, and river ice produced an ice jam on January 23rd. At 330 PM the river came out of banks in Eastlake. The first location to flood was Trader Jacks Riverside Grille. At approximately 1000 PM the river rose to about 15 feet with large ice chunks. The flood waters inundated West Island and Admiral Drive in Eastlake, resulting in swift water rescues. In all six families needed rescue. Erie Road was also cut off by floodwaters and ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following several days of showers and thunderstorms, a final upper level disturbance crossed on the 31st bringing another round of showers and storms. A couple of the storms produced hail. The ground was wet enough that the additional 0.5 to 1.0 inch of rain that fell on the 31st caused flash flooding in the late morning and early afternoon. This led to areal flooding during the late afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed due to flooding. State Route 356 was closed due to flooding along Raccoon Creek and Onion Creek. Elk Fork flooded, closing a section of State Route 677. Flooding along Wheelabout Creek closed a portion of State Route 278.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pre frontal trough and remnant outflow boundaries allowed convection to develop. Moderate to extreme instability was in place with SBCAPE over 3500 J/KG and DCAPE around 1500 J/KG. However, shear profiles were marginal. More concentrated damage occurred in a few spots in northwestern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ottawa-Putnam County Airport AWOS recorded a 62 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms moved out of northern Indiana into northwestern Ohio where limited heating allowed for some intensification of the complex. One thunderstorm produced a wet microburst in Van Wert county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported eight trees, approximately 80 feet tall, were damaged. A few were snapped half way up the trunk with lots of smaller limbs on the ground. All fell towards the southeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow boundaries from convection across central Lower Michigan and subtle shortwaves passing through the region caused thunderstorms to develop. High CAPE values but low shear allowed the storms to form quickly but struggle to organize. Eventually, greater DCAPE values arrive with one of the waves and caused more extensive wind damage in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a large tree branch was blown down at the intersection of CR 10 and R.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms pushed south across northern and central Ohio during the overnight hours ahead of a slow moving cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to four inches of rain caused flash flooding in the Montzuma area. State Route 127 was closed due to high water. Numerous houses and trailers in the area of Karafit Road were completely flooded and many roads were impassable. Several vehicles were stranded in high water, leading to a few water rescues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow boundaries from convection across central Lower Michigan and subtle shortwaves passing through the region caused thunderstorms to develop. High CAPE values but low shear allowed the storms to form quickly but struggle to organize. Eventually, greater DCAPE values arrive with one of the waves and caused more extensive wind damage in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported trees and power lines were blown down. The doors from a barn were also removed.

A strong low pressure system moved from the Plains into the western Great Lakes on the 14th. This pushed a cold front through the middle Ohio River Valley that evening into the early hours of the 15th. Thunderstorms formed ahead of this front and tapped into very strong winds aloft to produce isolated tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down across State Route 93 near Pedro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pre frontal trough and remnant outflow boundaries allowed convection to develop. Moderate to extreme instability was in place with SBCAPE over 3500 J/KG and DCAPE around 1500 J/KG. However, shear profiles were marginal. More concentrated damage occurred in a few spots in northwestern Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported large tree limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle and freezing rain developed during the afternoon and early evening hours of January 22nd creating slick roads. Ice accretions of a glaze to one tenth of an inch were common across far northwest Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and drizzle developed late in the afternoon on January 22nd and continued into the evening. Temperatures did warm above freezing into the morning of January 23rd while the rain continued, though sub-freezing road temperatures kept most secondary roads and sidewalks slick into January 23rd. There were numerous reports of slide-offs and accidents across the region. Many schools and businesses across the region were closed due to these icy road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system through the Ohio Valley produced a swath of accumulating snow and strong winds across the region on Saturday, January 19th. Snow totals of 4 to 8 inches and wind gusts to near 40 mph created difficult travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to at times heavy snow developed during the morning hours of January 19th and continued through the rest of the day. Winds really pick up during the day, gusting between 35 and 45 mph during the afternoon and evening hours. Snow accumulations across the county generally ranged between 6 and 9 inches, with significant blowing and drifting reported due to the strong winds. Lima received 7.0 of total snow accumulation. Slide-offs and accidents were reported across the region. A few secondary roads were impassable due to the significant drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes region on the morning of January 29 and departed into Canada. In the wake of this low, an arctic cold front moved through the lower Great Lakes region early on January 29, allowing for arctic high pressure to build over the midwest, setting up an extremely cold pattern for January 29 - 31. The blistering cold that entered the region resulted in temperatures on January 30 and 31 being some of the coldest recorded in five years. A strong pressure gradient set up over the region between the departing low pressure system and the incoming surface high resulting in winds to 30 - 35 mph across the region. These strong winds along with the extreme cold allowed for wind chills to drop below zero degrees by midday on January 29 and not return above zero until late on January 31. The coldest temperatures during this event were on the morning of January 30 with several locations approaching or exceeding record low temperatures in the -5F to -11F range. During these coldest temperatures, the wind chill reached -25F for several hours across the entire area with several locations reaching wind chill values of -35F to -40F for a time. Temperatures on January 30 stayed near or below zero for much of the day and dropped back below zero on the morning of January 31. Record low temperatures were set again on January 31, but the winds had subsided to 15 to 20 mph across the area, allowing for wind chills in the -15F to -20F range. Temperatures reached the upper single digits during the day on January 31 and winds subsided to light and variable, ending the extreme cold threat. Four deaths were reported in Ohio as a result of the extreme cold. All area schools closed on both January 30 and 31 due to the cold conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely cold weather settled over the area on January 30 and 31, bringing the coldest temperatures that the region had seen in four or five years. With winds gusting to 30 - 35 mph, wind chills across the county dropped below zero midday on January 29 and did not recover to above zero until late on January 31. Wind chills reached -25F for a period on January 30 with wind chills of -30F to -35F being noted during the late morning hours. All area schools were closed on January 30 and 31 due to the extreme cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front south of the Ohio River became a focus for deeper moisture over the area, which got wrung out as light accumulations of snow when an upper level shortwave crossed the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer 6 miles east of West Union measured 2 inches of snow, as did the county garage southwest of West Union.

All area schools were closed on January 30 and 31 due to the extreme cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes region on the morning of January 29 and departed into Canada. In the wake of this low, an arctic cold front moved through the lower Great Lakes region early on January 29, allowing for arctic high pressure to build over the midwest, setting up an extremely cold pattern for January 29 - 31. The blistering cold that entered the region resulted in temperatures on January 30 and 31 being some of the coldest recorded in five years. A strong pressure gradient set up over the region between the departing low pressure system and the incoming surface high resulting in winds to 30 - 35 mph across the region. These strong winds along with the extreme cold allowed for wind chills to drop below zero degrees by midday on January 29 and not return above zero until late on January 31. The coldest temperatures during this event were on the morning of January 30 with several locations approaching or exceeding record low temperatures in the -5F to -11F range. During these coldest temperatures, the wind chill reached -25F for several hours across the entire area with several locations reaching wind chill values of -35F to -40F for a time. Temperatures on January 30 stayed near or below zero for much of the day and dropped back below zero on the morning of January 31. Record low temperatures were set again on January 31, but the winds had subsided to 15 to 20 mph across the area, allowing for wind chills in the -15F to -20F range. Temperatures reached the upper single digits during the day on January 31 and winds subsided to light and variable, ending the extreme cold threat. Four deaths were reported in Ohio as a result of the extreme cold. All area schools closed on both January 30 and 31 due to the cold conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely cold weather settled over the area on January 30 and 31, bringing the coldest temperatures that the region had seen in four or five years. Low temperatures at Toledo Express Airport reached -10F on both January 30 and 31, setting new record lows for each date. With winds gusting to 30 - 35 mph, wind chills across the county dropped below zero midday on January 29 and did not recover to above zero until late on January 31. At Toledo Express Airport, wind chills reached -25F for a period on January 30 and the lowest wind chill recorded during the event was -38F during the late morning hours of the 30th. All area schools were closed on January 30 and 31 due to the extreme cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low over the Southern Plains on January 18 deepened as it tracked northeast to the lower Ohio Valley on January 19, eventually reaching the upper Ohio Valley early on January 20. Heavy snow associated with this low entered northwest Ohio early Saturday morning and quickly spread east across northern Ohio by early afternoon on the 19th. As this low pressure moved northeast during this time, some warm air entered the area and allowed for some freezing rain and sleet to develop in areas along and south of US Highway 30 in Ohio for some time in the afternoon on the 19th. Up to 0.15��� of freezing rain and an inch or two of sleet was observed in these areas before switching back to all snow. Meanwhile, much of northern Ohio had at least 6 inches of snow with many counties seeing an average of 8 to 10 inches of snow. Areas along Interstate 71 in north central Ohio and Interstate 76 in northeast Ohio had some of the highest snowfall amounts in the state with this system with many areas exceeding a foot of snow. Snow exited the area by the morning of the 20th but some lake effect bands developed over Northeast Ohio and added another 1 to 4 inches of snow, mostly across the secondary snowbelt. Winds during this event were strong with 15 to 25 mph sustained winds and occasional gusts to 35 to 40 mph. However, due to the density of the snow, visibilities across the area never settled under a half a mile for a long duration and an blizzard-like conditions were isolated, at best. The highest snowfall total in Ohio was in the Homerville area of Medina County, where almost 20 inches of snow was measured. Snowfall totals from area climate sites include: 14 inches at Mansfield Lahm Airport, 8.6 inches at Akron-Canton Airport, 8.5 inches at Youngstown-Warren Airport, 8 inches at Cleveland Hopkins Airport, and 4.7 inches at Toledo Express Airport. This snow event occurred over the MLK holiday weekend, allowing for generally minimal impacts, as schools were closed. Numerous businesses and churches were closed for the weekend. There were numerous accidents and road closures during this event and Level 2 and 3 Snow Emergencies were issued by many counties across the area. Luckily, there were no known injuries or fatalities due to the winter storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow spread into northwest Ohio during the morning hours of January 19, as low pressure moved into the Ohio Valley region. Snow was very light to start, but by the afternoon on the 19th moderate-to-heavy snow moved into the area with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches of snow per hour. Snowfall rates quickly lessened by the evening hours and snow ended over the area during the early morning hours of the 20th. Strong northeast to northwest winds accompanied the snow with gusts in excess of 25 mph for much of the storm, allowing for considerable blowing and drifting. Travel was severely disrupted by this storm. Roads in rural areas were nearly impassable at times. However, this snow event occurred over the MLK holiday weekend, allowing for generally minimal impacts, as schools were closed. Numerous businesses and churches were closed for the weekend. In Seneca County, snowfall totals ranged from 7 to 12 inches of snow for the event. A peak total of 12 inches was recorded several miles southeast of Tiffin. Other totals in the area include: 8.5 inches in Seneca Township and 7.3 inches in Tiffin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through northern Ohio on the evening of January 7th, allowing for an unseasonably warm day on January 8th with temperatures reaching the mid 50s by mid-morning. This allowed for thunderstorms to develop over north central Ohio by daybreak on the 8th, which produced widespread hail over the Cleveland and Akron metro areas. These storms continued east into Trumbull County where a rare January tornado formed. This EF-1 tornado damaged portions of Bazetta Township and the city of Cortland and moved over Mosquito Creek Lake. This tornado is the first January tornado in northern Ohio since January 5, 1997, when an F1 tornado hit portions of Wayne County. A second round of showers and thunderstorms developed over the region ahead of a cold front on the afternoon of the 8th. Widespread hail was observed with these thunderstorms, including several reports of hail one inch or greater in diameter. A couple of thunderstorms produced some wind damage in the eastern suburbs of Cleveland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of quarter-sized hail in the Elyria area from trained spotters and social media. Quarter-sized hail was reported near the Lorain County Office of Emergency Management and Homeland Security building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dangerous arctic air mass overspread northwest Ohio January 30th into the morning hours of January 31st. Wind chills between 25 and 45 degrees below zero were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty west winds ushered in a bitterly cold arctic air mass on January 30th into the morning hours of January 31st. The coldest of the air and strongest winds occurred during the morning hours of January 30th when the wind chill value at West Unity dropped to 35 degrees below zero. Wind chills through the event generally ranged between 25 and 45 degrees below zero. The coldest air temperature recorded at West Unity was 19 degrees below zero on January 30th. Many businesses and schools were closed on both January 30th and January 31st due to this record breaking shot of dangerous cold air.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme cold arctic air surged into the northeast region January 30th. 500mb heights were close to 5040 meters, 850mb temperatures ranged from -26 to -30 degrees Celsius, and 925mb temperatures were near -22 to -26 degrees Celsius. With the quick drop in temperature in the boundary layer, a steep low level lapse rate was created near the surface. Due to this steep lapse rate, winds near 850mb to 925mb (averaging 20kts) were capable of reaching the surface and created wind chill temperatures of -22F and lower.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind chill of -30.0 F was measured near Hanoverton, OH on Jan 30th.

The coldest of the air and strongest winds occurred during the morning hours of January 30th when the wind chill value at Fulton County Airport dropped to 39 degrees below zero. Wind chills through the event generally ranged between 25 and 45 degrees below zero. The coldest air temperature recorded at Archbold was 18 degrees below zero on January 30th. Many businesses and schools were closed on both January 30th and January 31st due to this record breaking shot of dangerous cold air.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through northern Ohio on the evening of January 7th, allowing for an unseasonably warm day on January 8th with temperatures reaching the mid 50s by mid-morning. This allowed for thunderstorms to develop over north central Ohio by daybreak on the 8th, which produced widespread hail over the Cleveland and Akron metro areas. These storms continued east into Trumbull County where a rare January tornado formed. This EF-1 tornado damaged portions of Bazetta Township and the city of Cortland and moved over Mosquito Creek Lake. This tornado is the first January tornado in northern Ohio since January 5, 1997, when an F1 tornado hit portions of Wayne County. A second round of showers and thunderstorms developed over the region ahead of a cold front on the afternoon of the 8th. Widespread hail was observed with these thunderstorms, including several reports of hail one inch or greater in diameter. A couple of thunderstorms produced some wind damage in the eastern suburbs of Cleveland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of pennies covered the ground about 3 miles northwest of New London.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system moved through the Ohio River Valley and central Appalachians on the 19th and 20th. Temperatures ahead of this system were warm, so precipitation started as rain. Around one inch of rain fell on the 19th. A cold front moved through late that evening, with temperatures dropping very quickly behind the front. This changed the precipitation over to a wintry mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain before becoming all snow early on the 20th. ||The rain ahead of the system turned mostly into runoff due to already saturated soils. This caused minor flooding on some creeks and streams.||Heavy snow fell early on the 20th, with 6-7 inches near New Lexington in Perry County.  In Morgan County, McConnelsville received 4 inches of snow. Amounts tapered off to the south and west with only 1-2 inches along the Ohio River.  In Jackson County, a 34-year-old woman was killed in a single vehicle accident on Vega Road. The vehicle she was driving slid off the side of an ice covered road, dropped over an embankment and ended up on its top in a creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through northern Ohio on the evening of January 7th, allowing for an unseasonably warm day on January 8th with temperatures reaching the mid 50s by mid-morning. This allowed for thunderstorms to develop over north central Ohio by daybreak on the 8th, which produced widespread hail over the Cleveland and Akron metro areas. These storms continued east into Trumbull County where a rare January tornado formed. This EF-1 tornado damaged portions of Bazetta Township and the city of Cortland and moved over Mosquito Creek Lake. This tornado is the first January tornado in northern Ohio since January 5, 1997, when an F1 tornado hit portions of Wayne County. A second round of showers and thunderstorms developed over the region ahead of a cold front on the afternoon of the 8th. Widespread hail was observed with these thunderstorms, including several reports of hail one inch or greater in diameter. A couple of thunderstorms produced some wind damage in the eastern suburbs of Cleveland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down in the far northeast corner of Champion Township near the intersection of N Park Avenue and Airport Road NW. This tornado continued east along Everett Hull Road in Bazetta Township, where it brought down numerous trees and power lines, including near the Trumbull County Fairgrounds. The tornado emerged over Mosquito Creek Lake and continued moving east returning over land at Hillside Cemetery in Cortland. At the cemetery, a storage shed was destroyed, shingles were blown off a chapel roof, and 36 headstones were overturned. Dozens of trees and bushes were also knocked down and the cemetery did not reopen until mid-February 2019 due to storm damage. From Hillside Cemetery, the tornado continued east into Cortland where damage to buildings, vehicles, and trees was noted. The tornado lifted before it reached High Street (Ohio State Route 5). The tornado was on the ground for about 4.5 miles and had a maximum width of 75 yards.

Severe thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and an isolated tornado across northern Ohio. These storms were developed by a strong cold front moving through the region and an unusually warm airmass for this time of year.

A low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes on the 23rd and 24th. This brought a prolonged period of light to moderate rain to the middle Ohio River Valley. This rain fell on several inches of snowpack, melting it through the day on the 23rd. Generally an inch of rain fell, which combined with the snow melt and created minor flooding across southeast Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Duck Creek flooded in central Washington County.||The stream gage on the West Fork of Duck Creek at Macksburg showed the creek rose out of its banks just after midnight on the 24th. It crested around 14.1 feet, or 1.1 feet above its bankfull level of 13 feet, mid afternoon. The creek returned to its banks shortly after midnight on the 25th. This flooded several low spots along the creek.||The stream gage on Duck Creek at Whipple showed the creek rose above its 12 foot bankfull level on the afternoon of the 24th. The creek crested half a foot above bankfull that evening and returned to it banks early on the 25th. This caused flooding in spots along State Routes 26, 145, and 821.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure in the Mississippi Valley tracked northeast through the Ohio Valley. A strong surface low developed in eastern Kentucky. Mixed freezing rain and sleet early on on the evening of the 19th quickly transitioned to a heavy snow overnight. This system was followed by bitter cold and sub-zero wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer in Fort Loramie measured 5 inches of snow, while another in Lockington measured 4.4 inches. Nearby observations and reports showed that significant disruptions resulted from this storm, along with locally higher snowfall totals.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved into the Pittsburgh region from the southeast U.S. and brought a wintry mix across our warning county area. On the east side of the low, warm air traveled as far north as Southern Butler County. As a result, areas near I-80 got 8 to 11 inches of snow while areas 50 miles south got sleet, rain, and freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer measured 11.0 inches of snow for a storm total on the morning of January 20th. Nine inches of snow was measured near Lisbon by the Department of Highways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A panhandle hook low pressure system moved northeast into the Great Lakes region on December 31, deepening as it moved north of Lake Erie on the evening of the 31st. This low extended a cold front across northeast Ohio, early on the 1st. Strong winds along and behind this front downed trees and power lines across the area, prompting widespread power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An automated weather station at Cleveland Hopkins International Airport measured a wind gust of 55 mph. Another automated weather station at Burke Lakefront Airport measured a wind gust of 53 mph. Several trees were reported downed in the southern part of the county, specifically, in North Royalton and Broadview Heights. Tens of thousands were without power into the morning of the 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level system produced a period of accumulating snow on Saturday January 12th. Snowfall amounts ranged between 3 and 5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed during the late morning hours of January 12th and continued into the afternoon and early evening. Snow amounts of 3 to 5 inches were common across the county. There were reports of minor accidents and slide-offs across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface warm front and quick moving mid level disturbance brought snow to the middle Ohio River Valley on the 1st. Arctic air was in place ahead of this system, leading to very efficient snowfall which quickly accumulated on the ground. By mid morning, 7 inches of snow had accumulated around New Lexington in Perry County. In Morgan County, 6.5 inches of snow fell near Eagleport and 6 inches of snow was measured near McConnelsville. The cooperative observer at Beverly, in Washington County, received 4.5 inches. There was a very sharp decrease in snow amounts headed south, with only 1-3 inches of snow along and south of Route 50, where more precipitation fell as rain.

A stationary front stalled across central Ohio on the afternoon of the 16th. Warm moist air was in place with dew points near 70 degrees. Recent rains across the region had produced nearly saturated ground conditions supporting rapid runoff and an increased risk of flash flooding. A flash flood watch was in effect for the afternoon hours. A mescoscale convective complex with heavy showers over Indiana moved east into the region in the early afternoon. Areas along and south of US 30 experienced the most flood impacts from this system as it produced 2 to 3 inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Swift water lead to road closures in Lucas near Wallace Road and Mt. Zion Road due to flooding from the Black Fork. 2.19 inches of rain fell during the evening and overnight hours. Water rescue of a man in a wheelchair was washed off the street by rushing waters and into a residential backyard where he was trapped for several hours.

Highway 250 near Jeromesville flooded and SR 302. Grandpa's Cheesebarn and Sweeties Chocolates closed due to flooding. Flooding at residences reported in Ashland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep freeze in late January produced thick river ice across the region. A rapid warm up in early February with temperatures in the 50s across the Maumee Basin, accompanied by snowmelt and rain combined runoff lead to ice break up and resultant ice jams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: River ice began to break up on the 5th with jamming reported just south of Toledo. Rain on the 6th and 7th measuring around 0.75��� across the basin caused river rises. Flooding attributed to the ice jam was reported between the riverside communities of Waterville north to Maumee. Impacts were localized to the Side Cut Metropark which cut off one popular commuter road Riverside Dr and Jerome Rd. Damages were reported to Riverside Cemetery and to the Metropark picnic areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report from Vandalia had 6 inches of snow. A social media post from west of Troy had 5.9 inches of snow. The CoCoRaHS observer from Piqua measured 5.7 inches. A spotter north of Troy reported 5.1 inches of snow, while a public report from Troy had 5 inches of accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front supported widespread showers and thunderstorms across northern and central Ohio. During the overnight of June 16th into the morning of the 17th heavy showers developed along this boundary. Warm and humid conditions with precipitable water values around 1.75-2.0��� supported very efficient rainfall with rainfall rates exceeding 3 inches per hour. Three day totals in this area were 3 to 6 inches with saturated ground conditions and already swollen rivers and creeks. The National Weather Service had a flood watch in effect for the entire region.  Numerous flash floods on the 16th and continued wet ground conditions supported rapid runoff with the rainfall during the early morning of the 17th. The additional rainfall 1.5 to 2.5 inches triggered flash floods and significant river flooding across the Tuscarawas watershed. Hundreds of homes and businesses in southern Summit County were flooded in Norton, Barberton, Clinton, New Franklin, and Copley Township. Canal Fulton (Stark County), Rittman (Medina and Wayne County), Chippewa Lake (Medina County), and Massillon (Stark County) also sustained major damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding at Wadsworth Airport after receiving 5.28��� of rain over 3 days, 4.20��� in 2 hours ending on the morning of the 17th. At least one home sustained significant flood damage. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration high wind event occurred across the area on February 24th. A low pressure system tracked out of the southern Plains and rapidly deepened as it moved towards the Great Lakes Region. This low originated over the panhandle of Texas on the 23rd with central pressure of 998 mb. The low tracked northeast to near Chicago by late on the 23rd, lifting a warm front across northern Ohio. The low continued northeast towards eastern portions of the Upper Peninsula of Michigan, deepening to 974 mb by mid-day on the 24th. This low extended a cold front over northern Ohio, moving west to east, and brought very strong southwest to westerly winds to the region during the morning hours on the 24th. Strong winds behind the cold front began in Northwest Ohio around 8 AM and reached Northeast Ohio just before Noon. Wind gusts of 50-60 mph were common through the afternoon and into the evening with some locations experiencing gusts of over 60 mph. Some of the peak wind reports included 67 mph at Cleveland Hopkins and Lorain County Airports, 64 mph at Toledo Express Airport, 63 mph at Findlay Airport, 61 mph at Akron-Canton, Burke Lakefront, Wayne County, and Youngstown-Warren Airports, 60 mph at Mansfield Lahm Airport, 59 mph at Erie-Ottawa and Toledo Executive Airports, and 58 mph at Cuyahoga County Airport.||The persistent battering of the very strong winds resulted in thousands of power outages to residents across the area. Over 200,000 power outages were reported across Ohio. Damage to trees were common, as large limbs were snapped or whole trees uprooted, exasperated by wet soil conditions that allowed trees to be toppled easily. In some cases trees fell on homes, power wires, and other structures. At least 2 semi-trucks were flipped on area roadways and a ban on empty semi-trucks was issued prior to the onset of strong winds for the Ohio Turnpike and I-90 by the Ohio Department of Transportation. Structural damage from the wind was also reported to numerous structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were downed across the area, including in North Canton and Massillon. Scattered power outages were reported in the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front supported widespread showers and thunderstorms across northern and central Ohio. During the overnight of June 16th into the morning of the 17th heavy showers developed along this boundary. Warm and humid conditions with precipitable water values around 1.75-2.0��� supported very efficient rainfall with rainfall rates exceeding 3 inches per hour. Three day totals in this area were 3 to 6 inches with saturated ground conditions and already swollen rivers and creeks. The National Weather Service had a flood watch in effect for the entire region.  Numerous flash floods on the 16th and continued wet ground conditions supported rapid runoff with the rainfall during the early morning of the 17th. The additional rainfall 1.5 to 2.5 inches triggered flash floods and significant river flooding across the Tuscarawas watershed. Hundreds of homes and businesses in southern Summit County were flooded in Norton, Barberton, Clinton, New Franklin, and Copley Township. Canal Fulton (Stark County), Rittman (Medina and Wayne County), Chippewa Lake (Medina County), and Massillon (Stark County) also sustained major damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bailey Lakes Fire Department reports several locations of Route 511 flooded and impassable near Nakin. Three water rescues conducted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front supported widespread showers and thunderstorms across northern and central Ohio. During the overnight of June 16th into the morning of the 17th heavy showers developed along this boundary. Warm and humid conditions with precipitable water values around 1.75-2.0��� supported very efficient rainfall with rainfall rates exceeding 3 inches per hour. Three day totals in this area were 3 to 6 inches with saturated ground conditions and already swollen rivers and creeks. The National Weather Service had a flood watch in effect for the entire region.  Numerous flash floods on the 16th and continued wet ground conditions supported rapid runoff with the rainfall during the early morning of the 17th. The additional rainfall 1.5 to 2.5 inches triggered flash floods and significant river flooding across the Tuscarawas watershed. Hundreds of homes and businesses in southern Summit County were flooded in Norton, Barberton, Clinton, New Franklin, and Copley Township. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Barberton is at the headwaters of the Tuscarawas watershed and receives water from 3 additional tributaries; the Wolf Creek, Mud Run, and Hudson Run. On the morning of the 17th these basins received an estimated 3 inches of. The heaviest rain fell after 4 AM with severe flooding starting by 5 AM. June was a particularly wet month with around 2 inches of rain having fallen on the 16th. This enhanced runoff and made already swollen creeks overrun their banks on the 17th.  Barberton is in a low area, so in addition to the rain the city receives, rain from surrounding areas flows into it, slowly putting the small city underwater. Up to three feet of 04water submerged some streets and up to six feet flooded many basements. The most significant impacts were from the Wolf Creek which flows south through Barberton. Flooding on 14th Street Northwest had up to 6 feet of water in their basements. Barber Road off ramp from I-76 was closed due to flooding. Miller Road between Brecksville and Barr Road, another main thruway was closed due to flooding. Several home and business owners were evacuated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure tracked across the middle Ohio Valley along a nearly stationary boundary, a fast-moving upper level shortwave provided the support for widespread snowfall. Most of the higher snow totals of 4 inches or more were recorded south of Interstate 70. The higher terrain of West Virginia and Maryland received better dynamic support from a crossing jet streak, providing better lift in a moist dendritic growth zone. Resulting snow-to-liquid ratios of 20:1 to 25:1 helped to create snow totals reaching double digits in a few cases.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county Department of Transportation office recorded 5.5 inches near Woodsfield. A trained spotter received 5 inches at Beallsville, while a member of the public reported 5 inches at Cameron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front supported widespread showers and thunderstorms across northern and central Ohio. During the overnight of June 16th into the morning of the 17th heavy showers developed along this boundary. Warm and humid conditions with precipitable water values around 1.75-2.0��� supported very efficient rainfall with rainfall rates exceeding 3 inches per hour. Three day totals in this area were 3 to 6 inches with saturated ground conditions and already swollen rivers and creeks. The National Weather Service had a flood watch in effect for the entire region.  Numerous flash floods on the 16th and continued wet ground conditions supported rapid runoff with the rainfall during the early morning of the 17th. The additional rainfall 1.5 to 2.5 inches triggered flash floods and significant river flooding across the Tuscarawas watershed. Hundreds of homes and businesses in southern Summit County were flooded in Norton, Barberton, Clinton, New Franklin, and Copley Township. Canal Fulton (Stark County), Rittman (Medina and Wayne County), Chippewa Lake (Medina County), and Massillon (Stark County) also sustained major damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pigeon Creek flooded homes near Springfield and Knox avenues in northwest Akron and Copley Township. Pigeon Creek inundated near 100 year, or 1 percent recurrence flood levels on the 17th. Pigeon Creek in eastern Barberton and northwest Akron received and estimated 5 inches of rain and resulted in over 100 year, or 1 percent recurrence flood levels.  Akron Canton airport measured 4.63��� of rain on the morning of the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front supported widespread showers and thunderstorms across northern and central Ohio. During the overnight of June 16th into the morning of the 17th heavy showers developed along this boundary. Warm and humid conditions with precipitable water values around 1.75-2.0��� supported very efficient rainfall with rainfall rates exceeding 3 inches per hour. Three day totals in this area were 3 to 6 inches with saturated ground conditions and already swollen rivers and creeks. The National Weather Service had a flood watch in effect for the entire region.  Numerous flash floods on the 16th and continued wet ground conditions supported rapid runoff with the rainfall during the early morning of the 17th. The additional rainfall 1.5 to 2.5 inches triggered flash floods and significant river flooding across the Tuscarawas watershed. Hundreds of homes and businesses in southern Summit County were flooded in Norton, Barberton, Clinton, New Franklin, and Copley Township. Canal Fulton (Stark County), Rittman (Medina and Wayne County), Chippewa Lake (Medina County), and Massillon (Stark County) also sustained major damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chippewa Creek, a tributary for the Tuscarawas River measured an estimated 7.34��� between the 11th and 17th of June, again with 3 to 5 inches falling on the 16th and 17th.  Heavy rain over the headwaters of the Tuscarwas River were averaged 7.65��� for from June 11 through the 17th, with 3 to 5 inches falling on the 16th and 17th. The Tuscarawas River basin average rainfall for June 11 through June 17th was 7.58��� with 3 to 5��� falling during the 16th and 17th. As a result of concurrent major flooding on the tributary Chippewa Creek and the headwaters of the Tuscarawas River resulted in a flood near the 0.2 percent or 500 year flood level at the confluence in Canal Fulton and Clinton. Downstream of Canal Fulton the Tuscarawas River reached a near 1 percent recurrence flood, or 100 year event in the City of Massillon.|The Chippewa Creek, a headwater to the Tuscwarawa River in Medina and Wayne Counties, came out of its banks on the 16th. The additional rain on the 17th cause the river to overtop the gage, with a height estimated at 10.53 feet around 10 am on the 17th. The USGS field crew was able to estimate flows exceeding 1450 cfs, making it the most significant flood since the gage was installed in 2001. Rainfall estimates for the watershed were 7.34��� between the June 11th and 17th, with 3 to 5 inches falling on the 16th and 17th. The Chippewa Creek confluence with the Tuscarawas River occurs in Rittman in Wayne County. Rittman sustained significant damages from the flooded Chippewa Creek.||In Clinton the flood waters at the confluence of the Chippewa Creek and Tuscarawas River produced between a 1 and a 0.2 percent flood levels, also known respectively as a 100 and 500 year flood plain. The majority of the impacts occurred along Main Street and North Street west of Donnenwirth Street with two to three feet deep on part of Main Street and six to eight feet deep on North Street. Most of the floodplain was part of the Erie Canal park system and trails, but at least 5 homes were surrounded and partially inundated by flood waters. Two businesses on Main Street did have an estimated 2 feet of water on the first floor, one of which was an auto repair shop with about 20 vehicles inundated with up to 3 feet of water. 25 people have been forced out of their homes or businesses. Flood levels were the worst since the flood of 1913, exceeding the floods of 1969 and 1977. Luna Lake levels rose by almost 4 feet in the month of June, resulting in widespread flooding of over a dozen homes and cabins along the lakeshore during the week of the 18th resulting the worst flooding since 1978.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the Plains to the upper Midwest on February 20th, before transitioning to a coastal system by the morning of the 21st. This low pushed a warm front northward across our region. Widespread precipitation was generated by deep moisture and an area of strong lift that accompanied the warm front. Precipitation started mainly as snow, but transitioned to a mostly brief period of mixed precipitation as warmer air moved in aloft. By late evening on the 20th, the passage of the warm front allowed all precipitation to change to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 2.2 inches of snow at St. Clairsville, and a trained spotter reported 2 inches at Bellaire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front supported widespread showers and thunderstorms across northern and central Ohio. During the overnight of June 16th into the morning of the 17th heavy showers developed along this boundary. Warm and humid conditions with precipitable water values around 1.75-2.0��� supported very efficient rainfall with rainfall rates exceeding 3 inches per hour. Three day totals in this area were 3 to 6 inches with saturated ground conditions and already swollen rivers and creeks. The National Weather Service had a flood watch in effect for the entire region.  Numerous flash floods on the 16th and continued wet ground conditions supported rapid runoff with the rainfall during the early morning of the 17th. The additional rainfall 1.5 to 2.5 inches triggered flash floods and significant river flooding across the Tuscarawas watershed. Hundreds of homes and businesses in southern Summit County were flooded in Norton, Barberton, Clinton, New Franklin, and Copley Township. Canal Fulton (Stark County), Rittman (Medina and Wayne County), Chippewa Lake (Medina County), and Massillon (Stark County) also sustained major damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the headwaters of the Tuscarwas River were averaged 7.65��� for from June 11 through the 17th, with 3 to 5 inches falling on the 16th and 17th. The Tuscarawas River basin average rainfall for June 11 through June 17th was 7.58��� with 3 to 5��� falling during the 16th and 17th. As a result of concurrent major flooding on the tributary Chippewa Creek and the headwaters of the Tuscarawas River resulted in a flood near the 0.2 percent or 500 year flood level at the confluence in Canal Fulton and Clinton. Downstream of Canal Fulton the Tuscarawas River reached a near 1 percent recurrence flood, or 100 year event in the City of Massillon.||In Canal Fulton, just downstream of Clinton, flood levels were higher than a 1 percent or 100 year flood, but less than a .2 percent or 500 year flood level. It was the worst since 1913 flood levels. Most of the flood waters were captured between the railroad tracks to the west and inundated mainly recreational and park areas on the east side of the river. At least 3 home had inundation on a first floor, dozens of others had inundation of basements. One neighborhood of 30 homes was cut off due to flooding on the only access road Millhaven Road. These homes were cut off for about a week before flood waters receded enough to allow passage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front stalled across central Ohio on the afternoon of the 16th. Warm moist air was in place with dew points near 70 degrees. Recent rains across the region had produced nearly saturated ground conditions supporting rapid runoff and an increased risk of flash flooding. A flash flood watch was in effect for the afternoon hours. A mescoscale convective complex with heavy showers over Indiana moved east into the region in the early afternoon. Areas along and south of US 30 experienced the most flood impacts from this system as it produced 2 to 3 inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Polk Law enforcement reported high water and impassable roads on route 89 and route 800 near Polk. Redhaw Creek flooding washed out a portion of Route 800. Route 700 near Wolf Run Creek was impassable due to high, swift water over the road. County Road 175 south of the town of Redhaw was impassable due to swift water over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of snow, freezing drizzle, and sleet had a significant impact on the February 20th morning commute. Light snow and ice accumulations were common across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, freezing drizzle and sleet created icy roads and sidewalks during the morning hours of February 20th. Numerous schools and some businesses were closed. There were also reports of slide-offs and accidents across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary in the area interacted with a modest mid level disturbance during the afternoon and evening on June 1st. MLCape values approached 1000 J/KG and Bulk shear values were up to 40 knots. Scattered severe thunderstorms developed with large hail. Later in the evening, several severe thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts and damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado touched down on North Columbus Street. The tornado lifted and touched down again two more time on Harding Way East and on 2nd Street. The tornado path was a little over a mile long. There was minor damage to outbuildings and tree tops snapped.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: State of Emergency declared in Rittman due to flooding on June 17.  Flash flooding stranded cars and closed the city���s only grocery store in Rittman. Around 5 a.m. the Landis Ditch, which runs past the Rittman Shopping Center, breached its banks and quickly flooded the above streets and flooded the businesses in the plaza including the Rittman IGA. The Chippewa Creek also exceeded its banks at a 100 year level, about 8 feet above normal. Water was approximately 3.5 feet deep on Whitman Road between Porr and the railroad tracks. Numerous vehicles stalled in flood waters and needed water rescues. Most flood damages occurred along Main Street and along Industrial and Ohio avenues by Marathon, with the worst areas landing around Seneca and State streets. A couple families were cut off from their homes as a result of the flooding. Flood waters receded by 1130 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system developed across the western Great Lakes on Sunday, February 24th. Widespread 30 to 40 mph prevailing west winds occurred with gusts of 45 to 68 mph. These high winds were of long duration (approximately 13-16 hours) and this was considered unprecedented based on local climatology. Numerous reports of downed large trees, large tree limbs, and utility poles were received along with many power outages across the entire area. The highest wind gust of the event was at KAOH with a 68 mph gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long duration sustained winds of 35 to 45 mph with gusts over 60 mph occurred across the county, resulting in trees, tree limbs and power poles suffering damage. The Lima airport recorded a 63 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meteotsunami edge wave affected Lake Erie on the evening of April 14, 2019 and caused damage along the shoreline and flooding of homes.  The event occurred at approximately 7:17 PM EDT and affected the shores from Madison Township, Ohio to Conneaut, Ohio.  Eyewitnesses reported  a series of 3 waves that moved onshore and were approximately 10 to 12 feet high.  Debris lines on the shore confirmed the estimated wave heights.  The entire event lasted about 10 minutes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Geneva-on-the-Lake State Park marina sustained considerable damage to their docks inside of the well protected marina.  Water levels rose 4 feet causing damage to the docks.  The water level settled back down within about 10 minutes.  Damage to a marina in Conneaut was also similar to the Geneva-on-the-Lake marina.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CoCoRaHS observer west of Versailles measured 6 inches of snow. Other observers northwest of Bradford and west of Potsdam measured 5.2 and 5.1 inches, respectively. The county garage in Greenville measured 5.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low over the Southern Plains on January 18 deepened as it tracked northeast to the lower Ohio Valley on January 19, eventually reaching the upper Ohio Valley early on January 20. Heavy snow associated with this low entered northwest Ohio early Saturday morning and quickly spread east across northern Ohio by early afternoon on the 19th. As this low pressure moved northeast during this time, some warm air entered the area and allowed for some freezing rain and sleet to develop in areas along and south of US Highway 30 in Ohio for some time in the afternoon on the 19th. Up to 0.15��� of freezing rain and an inch or two of sleet was observed in these areas before switching back to all snow. Meanwhile, much of northern Ohio had at least 6 inches of snow with many counties seeing an average of 8 to 10 inches of snow. Areas along Interstate 71 in north central Ohio and Interstate 76 in northeast Ohio had some of the highest snowfall amounts in the state with this system with many areas exceeding a foot of snow. Snow exited the area by the morning of the 20th but some lake effect bands developed over Northeast Ohio and added another 1 to 4 inches of snow, mostly across the secondary snowbelt. Winds during this event were strong with 15 to 25 mph sustained winds and occasional gusts to 35 to 40 mph. However, due to the density of the snow, visibilities across the area never settled under a half a mile for a long duration and an blizzard-like conditions were isolated, at best. The highest snowfall total in Ohio was in the Homerville area of Medina County, where almost 20 inches of snow was measured. Snowfall totals from area climate sites include: 14 inches at Mansfield Lahm Airport, 8.6 inches at Akron-Canton Airport, 8.5 inches at Youngstown-Warren Airport, 8 inches at Cleveland Hopkins Airport, and 4.7 inches at Toledo Express Airport. This snow event occurred over the MLK holiday weekend, allowing for generally minimal impacts, as schools were closed. Numerous businesses and churches were closed for the weekend. There were numerous accidents and road closures during this event and Level 2 and 3 Snow Emergencies were issued by many counties across the area. Luckily, there were no known injuries or fatalities due to the winter storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow spread into northern Ohio during the morning hours of January 19, as low pressure moved into the Ohio Valley region. Snow was very light to start, but by the afternoon on the 19th moderate-to-heavy snow moved into the area with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches of snow per hour. Snowfall rates quickly lessened by the evening hours and snow lingered through the afternoon hours of the 20th. Strong northeast to northwest winds accompanied the snow with gusts in excess of 25 mph for much of the storm, allowing for considerable blowing and drifting. Travel was severely disrupted by this storm. Roads in rural areas were nearly impassable at times. However, this snow event occurred over the MLK holiday weekend, allowing for generally minimal impacts, as schools were closed. Numerous businesses and churches were closed for the weekend. In Ashland County, snowfall totals ranged from 8 to 13 inches of snow for the event. A peak total of 14 inches of snow was recorded near Sullivan. Reports of 8 to 12 inches of snow were noted for the Ashland area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed through the afternoon hours ahead of an approaching cold front. Some of the storms produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple power poles and power lines were downed across the county. Several barns were also damaged throughout the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto a house on Merwin 10 Mile Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed through the afternoon hours ahead of an approaching cold front. Some of the storms produced damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and power lines were downed at Bixby Road and Ebright Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving line of thunderstorms produced locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Middleboro Road was closed due to high water flowing across it. This was caused by Todd's Fork being out of its bank.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure lifted from the central Plains on the morning of the 23rd into the western Great Lakes by the 24th.  A cold front then rushed across the region during the late morning/early afternoon hours of the 24th.  Some rain showers accompanied the front, but the main story was the prolonged period of damaging wind gusts that followed the frontal passage. A very tight pressure gradient, 3 hour pressure rises on the order of 7-8 mb/hr, and deep mixing of up to 700 mb allowed for the efficient downward transport of strong wind aloft. The result was an extended period of 40-60 MPH wind gusts across the region.  Several reports of peak gusts of 60 MPH or greater were received.||The highest recorded gust was 74 MPH at a mesonet sensor east of Canaan Heights in Tucker County, WV.  A number of peak wind gusts in excess of 60 MPH were observed as well. There were widespread reports of downed trees, power lines, and structural damage across the entire region, with some examples noted in the county entries. Thankfully, no injuries were reported from wind effects.||Power outages were widespread as well.  An early estimate indicated over 200,000 outages in the state of Ohio. Full restoration took multiple days due to the extent of damage and the initially unsafe working conditions due to the ongoing wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county emergency manager stated that several trees and large branches were blown down in the New Philadelphia/Dover area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that several large trees were blown down 3 miles west of Lisbon. Another trained spotter reported multiple trees blown down and a power pole snapped near Hanoverton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes region on the morning of January 29 and departed into Canada. In the wake of this low, an arctic cold front moved through the lower Great Lakes region early on January 29, allowing for arctic high pressure to build over the midwest, setting up an extremely cold pattern for January 29 - 31. The blistering cold that entered the region resulted in temperatures on January 30 and 31 being some of the coldest recorded in five years. A strong pressure gradient set up over the region between the departing low pressure system and the incoming surface high resulting in winds to 30 - 35 mph across the region. These strong winds along with the extreme cold allowed for wind chills to drop below zero degrees by midday on January 29 and not return above zero until late on January 31. The coldest temperatures during this event were on the morning of January 30 with several locations approaching or exceeding record low temperatures in the -5F to -11F range. During these coldest temperatures, the wind chill reached -25F for several hours across the entire area with several locations reaching wind chill values of -35F to -40F for a time. Temperatures on January 30 stayed near or below zero for much of the day and dropped back below zero on the morning of January 31. Record low temperatures were set again on January 31, but the winds had subsided to 15 to 20 mph across the area, allowing for wind chills in the -15F to -20F range. Temperatures reached the upper single digits during the day on January 31 and winds subsided to light and variable, ending the extreme cold threat. Four deaths were reported in Ohio as a result of the extreme cold. All area schools closed on both January 30 and 31 due to the cold conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely cold weather settled over the area on January 30 and 31, bringing the coldest temperatures that the region had seen in four or five years. With winds gusting to 30 - 35 mph, wind chills across the county dropped below zero midday on January 29 and did not recover to above zero until late on January 31. Wind chills reached -25F for a period on January 30 with wind chills of -30F to -35F being noted during the late morning hours. All area schools were closed on January 30 and 31 due to the extreme cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front dropped through the middle Ohio River Valley on the 8th. Strong to severe thunderstorms along the front led to wind damage, hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof of a porch was blown off and several trees fell down near the Post Office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure lifted from the central Plains on the morning of the 23rd into the western Great Lakes by the 24th.  A cold front then rushed across the region during the late morning/early afternoon hours of the 24th.  Some rain showers accompanied the front, but the main story was the prolonged period of damaging wind gusts that followed the frontal passage. A very tight pressure gradient, 3 hour pressure rises on the order of 7-8 mb/hr, and deep mixing of up to 700 mb allowed for the efficient downward transport of strong wind aloft. The result was an extended period of 40-60 MPH wind gusts across the region.  Several reports of peak gusts of 60 MPH or greater were received.||The highest recorded gust was 74 MPH at a mesonet sensor east of Canaan Heights in Tucker County, WV.  A number of peak wind gusts in excess of 60 MPH were observed as well. There were widespread reports of downed trees, power lines, and structural damage across the entire region, with some examples noted in the county entries. Thankfully, no injuries were reported from wind effects.||Power outages were widespread as well.  An early estimate indicated over 200,000 outages in the state of Ohio. Full restoration took multiple days due to the extent of damage and the initially unsafe working conditions due to the ongoing wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Cambridge, Old Washington, and Birmingham, according to county law enforcement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes region on the morning of January 29 and departed into Canada. In the wake of this low, an arctic cold front moved through the lower Great Lakes region early on January 29, allowing for arctic high pressure to build over the midwest, setting up an extremely cold pattern for January 29 - 31. The blistering cold that entered the region resulted in temperatures on January 30 and 31 being some of the coldest recorded in five years. A strong pressure gradient set up over the region between the departing low pressure system and the incoming surface high resulting in winds to 30 - 35 mph across the region. These strong winds along with the extreme cold allowed for wind chills to drop below zero degrees by midday on January 29 and not return above zero until late on January 31. The coldest temperatures during this event were on the morning of January 30 with several locations approaching or exceeding record low temperatures in the -5F to -11F range. During these coldest temperatures, the wind chill reached -25F for several hours across the entire area with several locations reaching wind chill values of -35F to -40F for a time. Temperatures on January 30 stayed near or below zero for much of the day and dropped back below zero on the morning of January 31. Record low temperatures were set again on January 31, but the winds had subsided to 15 to 20 mph across the area, allowing for wind chills in the -15F to -20F range. Temperatures reached the upper single digits during the day on January 31 and winds subsided to light and variable, ending the extreme cold threat. Four deaths were reported in Ohio as a result of the extreme cold. All area schools closed on both January 30 and 31 due to the cold conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely cold weather settled over the area on January 30 and 31, bringing the coldest temperatures that the region had seen in four or five years. Low temperatures at Akron Fulton Airport reached -3F on January 30 and -2F on January 31. Low temperatures at Akron-Canton Airport reached -5F on January 30 and -3F on January 31. With winds gusting to 30 - 35 mph, wind chills across the county dropped below zero midday on January 29 and did not recover to above zero until late on January 31. At Akron Fulton and Akron-Canton Airports, wind chills reached -25F for a period on January 30. The lowest wind chill recorded during the event at Akron Fulton Airport was -26F and at Akron-Canton Airport it was -29F, both during the late morning hours of the 30th. All area schools were closed on January 30 and 31 due to the extreme cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low over the Southern Plains on January 18 deepened as it tracked northeast to the lower Ohio Valley on January 19, eventually reaching the upper Ohio Valley early on January 20. Heavy snow associated with this low entered northwest Ohio early Saturday morning and quickly spread east across northern Ohio by early afternoon on the 19th. As this low pressure moved northeast during this time, some warm air entered the area and allowed for some freezing rain and sleet to develop in areas along and south of US Highway 30 in Ohio for some time in the afternoon on the 19th. Up to 0.15��� of freezing rain and an inch or two of sleet was observed in these areas before switching back to all snow. Meanwhile, much of northern Ohio had at least 6 inches of snow with many counties seeing an average of 8 to 10 inches of snow. Areas along Interstate 71 in north central Ohio and Interstate 76 in northeast Ohio had some of the highest snowfall amounts in the state with this system with many areas exceeding a foot of snow. Snow exited the area by the morning of the 20th but some lake effect bands developed over Northeast Ohio and added another 1 to 4 inches of snow, mostly across the secondary snowbelt. Winds during this event were strong with 15 to 25 mph sustained winds and occasional gusts to 35 to 40 mph. However, due to the density of the snow, visibilities across the area never settled under a half a mile for a long duration and an blizzard-like conditions were isolated, at best. The highest snowfall total in Ohio was in the Homerville area of Medina County, where almost 20 inches of snow was measured. Snowfall totals from area climate sites include: 14 inches at Mansfield Lahm Airport, 8.6 inches at Akron-Canton Airport, 8.5 inches at Youngstown-Warren Airport, 8 inches at Cleveland Hopkins Airport, and 4.7 inches at Toledo Express Airport. This snow event occurred over the MLK holiday weekend, allowing for generally minimal impacts, as schools were closed. Numerous businesses and churches were closed for the weekend. There were numerous accidents and road closures during this event and Level 2 and 3 Snow Emergencies were issued by many counties across the area. Luckily, there were no known injuries or fatalities due to the winter storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow spread into northeast Ohio during the morning hours of January 19, as low pressure moved into the Ohio Valley region. Snow was very light to start. During the afternoon of the 19th, snow had even transitioned to some freezing rain as some warm air entered the region. However, by the late afternoon on the 19th, any mixed precipitation gave way to moderate-to-heavy snow that moved into the area with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches of snow per hour. Snowfall rates quickly lessened by the evening hours and snow lingered through the afternoon hours of the 20th. Strong northeast to northwest winds accompanied the snow with gusts in excess of 25 mph for much of the storm, allowing for considerable blowing and drifting. Travel was severely disrupted by this storm. Roads in rural areas were nearly impassable at times. However, this snow event occurred over the MLK holiday weekend, allowing for generally minimal impacts, as schools were closed. Numerous businesses and churches were closed for the weekend. In Stark County, snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 12 inches of snow for the event. A peak total of 12 inches of snow was recorded near Washington Township, south of Alliance. Other snow totals across the county include: 10 inches in Alliance and Jackson Township, 9 inches in Waynesburg, 8 inches in Massillon, 7 inches in Magnolia and East Canton, and 6 inches in Canton. In addition to the snow, up to a tenth of an inch of ice was recorded in the southwestern half of the county.

Record low temperatures were set again on January 31, but the winds had subsided to 15 to 20 mph across the area, allowing for wind chills in the -15F to -20F range. Temperatures reached the upper single digits during the day on January 31 and winds subsided to light and variable, ending the extreme cold threat. Four deaths were reported in Ohio as a result of the extreme cold. All area schools closed on both January 30 and 31 due to the cold conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely cold weather settled over the area on January 30 and 31, bringing the coldest temperatures that the region had seen in four or five years. Low temperatures at Knox County Airport reached -4F on January 30 and -2F on January 31. With winds gusting to 30 - 35 mph, wind chills across the county dropped below zero midday on January 29 and did not recover to above zero until late on January 31. At Knox County Airport, wind chills reached -25F for a period on January 30 and the lowest wind chill recorded during the event was -33F during the late morning hours of the 30th. All area schools were closed on January 30 and 31 due to the extreme cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes region on the morning of January 29 and departed into Canada. In the wake of this low, an arctic cold front moved through the lower Great Lakes region early on January 29, allowing for arctic high pressure to build over the midwest, setting up an extremely cold pattern for January 29 - 31. The blistering cold that entered the region resulted in temperatures on January 30 and 31 being some of the coldest recorded in five years. A strong pressure gradient set up over the region between the departing low pressure system and the incoming surface high resulting in winds to 30 - 35 mph across the region. These strong winds along with the extreme cold allowed for wind chills to drop below zero degrees by midday on January 29 and not return above zero until late on January 31. The coldest temperatures during this event were on the morning of January 30 with several locations approaching or exceeding record low temperatures in the -5F to -11F range. During these coldest temperatures, the wind chill reached -25F for several hours across the entire area with several locations reaching wind chill values of -35F to -40F for a time. Record low temperatures were set again on January 31, but the winds had subsided to 15 to 20 mph across the area, allowing for wind chills in the -15F to -20F range. Temperatures reached the upper single digits during the day on January 31 and winds subsided to light and variable, ending the extreme cold threat. Four deaths were reported in Ohio as a result of the extreme cold. All area schools closed on both January 30 and 31 due to the cold conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low over the Southern Plains on January 18 deepened as it tracked northeast to the lower Ohio Valley on January 19, eventually reaching the upper Ohio Valley early on January 20. Heavy snow associated with this low entered northwest Ohio early Saturday morning and quickly spread east across northern Ohio by early afternoon on the 19th. As this low pressure moved northeast during this time, some warm air entered the area and allowed for some freezing rain and sleet to develop in areas along and south of US Highway 30 in Ohio for some time in the afternoon on the 19th. Up to 0.15��� of freezing rain and an inch or two of sleet was observed in these areas before switching back to all snow. Meanwhile, much of northern Ohio had at least 6 inches of snow with many counties seeing an average of 8 to 10 inches of snow. Areas along Interstate 71 in north central Ohio and Interstate 76 in northeast Ohio had some of the highest snowfall amounts in the state with this system with many areas exceeding a foot of snow. Snow exited the area by the morning of the 20th but some lake effect bands developed over Northeast Ohio and added another 1 to 4 inches of snow, mostly across the secondary snowbelt. Winds during this event were strong with 15 to 25 mph sustained winds and occasional gusts to 35 to 40 mph. However, due to the density of the snow, visibilities across the area never settled under a half a mile for a long duration and an blizzard-like conditions were isolated, at best. The highest snowfall total in Ohio was in the Homerville area of Medina County, where almost 20 inches of snow was measured. Snowfall totals from area climate sites include: 14 inches at Mansfield Lahm Airport, 8.6 inches at Akron-Canton Airport, 8.5 inches at Youngstown-Warren Airport, 8 inches at Cleveland Hopkins Airport, and 4.7 inches at Toledo Express Airport. This snow event occurred over the MLK holiday weekend, allowing for generally minimal impacts, as schools were closed. Numerous businesses and churches were closed for the weekend. There were numerous accidents and road closures during this event and Level 2 and 3 Snow Emergencies were issued by many counties across the area. Luckily, there were no known injuries or fatalities due to the winter storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow spread into northwest Ohio during the morning hours of January 19, as low pressure moved into the Ohio Valley region. Snow was very light to start, but by the afternoon on the 19th moderate-to-heavy snow moved into the area with snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches of snow per hour. Snowfall rates quickly lessened by the evening hours and snow ended over the area during the early morning hours of the 20th. Strong northeast to northwest winds accompanied the snow with gusts in excess of 25 mph for much of the storm, allowing for considerable blowing and drifting. Travel was severely disrupted by this storm. Roads in rural areas were nearly impassable at times. However, this snow event occurred over the MLK holiday weekend, allowing for generally minimal impacts, as schools were closed. Numerous businesses and churches were closed for the weekend. In Ottawa County, snowfall totals averaged around 6 inches of snow for the event. Reports of 6 inches of snow were noted in Port Clinton and Put-In-Bay.
